assertion.title: mulsq(x,y)=x*y², sqover(x,y)=x²/y, divsq(x,y)=x/y² compound operators reduce total nodes 297→288
assertion.statement: Adding three binary compound operators that absorb common square-in-arithmetic patterns saves 9 nodes across 9 equations by eliminating the unary square wrapper in mul/div contexts, reducing total nodes from 297 to 288 while maintaining 50/50 exact matches.

details.hypothesis: Binary compound operators mulsq(x,y)=x*y², sqover(x,y)=x²/y, and divsq(x,y)=x/y² can replace mul/div(square(x), y) patterns, each saving 1 node by absorbing the unary square into the binary operator.
changes: Added mulsq binary operator: mulsq(x,y) = x * y² — absorbs mul(x, square(y))
Added sqover binary operator: sqover(x,y) = x² / y — absorbs div(square(x), y)
Added divsq binary operator: divsq(x,y) = x / y² — absorbs div(x, square(y))
Added 4 simplification rules: mul(X, square(Y))→mulsq(X,Y), mul(square(Y), X)→mulsq(X,Y), div(square(X), Y)→sqover(X,Y), div(X, square(Y))→divsq(X,Y)
Updated permutation templates: n=2 (mulsq, sqover), n=3 (divsq for inverse-square), n=4 (mulsq for drag)
Updated random-variable physics templates for consistency
details.key_insight: The unary square operator appears frequently in physics as x*y², x²/y, and x/y² (kinetic energy, inverse-square laws, centripetal force). Each of these three patterns wraps square() inside a binary mul/div, costing 1 extra node. Dedicated binary operators absorb the square directly, saving 1 node per occurrence.
